questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A function $F$ is defined by $F(x)=\int_1^x \frac{e^t}{t} d t \quad \forall\gt0$. Now express the functions in List-I in terms of $F$, then match the following List-I with List-II
questions.solution:
(P) Put t $+2=z$
(Q) Put $3 t=z$
(R) Integrate by part
(S) Put $\frac{1}{t}=z$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: If equation of column-I is given along with condition of column-II then match it with appropriate value given in column-III.
Question: Which of the following is incorrect combination?
questions.solution:
(I) $\because \quad \int_0^2 f(x) d x=\int_0^1 f(x) d x+\int_1^2 f(x) d x$
Let $x=2 t \Rightarrow d x=2 d t$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \int_0^1 f(2 t) d t=I+\int_1^2 f(x) d x$
$\therefore \quad$ If $f(2 x)=3 f(x)$ then $\int_1^2 f(x) d x=5 \Rightarrow$ (I) (ii) (P)
and also
(II) $\int_0^4 f(x) d x=\int_0^2 f(x) d x+\int_2^4 f(x) d x$
Let $x=2 t$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \int_0^2 f(2 t) d t=2+\int_2^4 f(x) d x$
$\Rightarrow$ If $f(2 x)=2 f(x)$ then $\int_2^4 f(x) d x=6 \Rightarrow$ (II) (i) (Q) and
$$
\because \quad \int_0^3 f(x) d x=\int_0^1 f(x) d x+\int_1^3 f(x) d x
$$

Let $x=3 t$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad 3 \int_0^1 f(3 t) d t=1+\int_1^3 f(x) d x
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ If $f(3 t)=3 f(t)$ then $\int^3 f(x) d x=8 \Rightarrow$ (I) (iii) (R)
and
$$
\int_0^9 f(x) d x=\int_0^3 f(x) d x+\int_3^9 f(x) d x
$$

Let $x=3 t$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad 3 \int_0^3 f(3 t) d t=\int_0^3 f(x) d x+\int_3^9 f(x) d x
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ If $f(3 t)=3 f(t)$ then $24=\int_3^9 f(x) d x \Rightarrow$ (III) (iii) (S)
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Matrix Matching
\(\begin{array}{l} \text{Column - I} & \text{Column-II} \\ \text{(A) } \int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1+x)\left(1+x^2\right)} d x \text{ is equal to} & \text{(p) } 3e \\ \begin{array}{l} \text{(B) If } f(x)=1+x+\int_1^x\left(\ln ^2 t+2 \ln t\right) d t. \\ \text{ Find the value of } f(a) \text{ where } f^{\prime}(a)=0 \end{array} & \text{(q) } 1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \begin{array}{l} \text{(C) Let } I_1=\int_0^1 \frac{e^x}{1+x} d x \text{ and } I_2=\int_0^1 \frac{x^2}{e^{x^3}\left(2-x^3\right)} d x, \\ \text{ then } \frac{I_1}{I_2} \text{ is equal to} \end{array} & \text{(r) } 1+2 \mathrm{e}^{-1} \\ \begin{array}{l} \text{(D) } \int_0^{\pi / 4} \sin (x-[x]) d(x-[x]) \text{ is equal to (where } \\ [.] \text{ denote greatest integer function of } x) \end{array} & \text{(s) } \frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \text{(t) } e^{-1} \end{array}\)
questions.solution:
(A) $\mathrm{I}=\int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1+\mathrm{x})\left(1+\mathrm{x}^2\right)} \mathrm{dx}$ put $\mathrm{x}=\tan \theta$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{I}=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sec ^2 \theta \mathrm{~d} \theta}{(1+\tan \theta) \sec ^2 \theta}=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{1+\tan \theta} d \theta \\
& I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta+\cos \theta} d \theta \\
& I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta+\sin \theta} d \theta \\
& 2 I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} 1 d \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \because \int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x \\
& I=\frac{\pi}{4}
\end{aligned}$
(B)
$\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=1+x+\int_1^x\left(\ln ^2 t+2 \ln t\right) d t \\
& f^{\prime}(x)=1+\ln ^2 x+2 \ln x \\
& f^{\prime}(a)=0 \Rightarrow(\ln a+1)^2=0 \Rightarrow a=e^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
So, $f(a)=f\left(e^{-1}\right)=1+e^{-1}+\int_1^{e^{-1}}\left(\ln ^2 t+2 \ln t\right) d t$
Put $\ln t=y \Rightarrow e^y=t \Rightarrow e^y d y=d t$
$\begin{aligned}
& f(a)=1+e^{-1}+\int_0^{-1} e^y\left(y^2+2 y\right) d y \\
& =1+e^{-1}+\left[e^y y^2\right]_0^{-1}=1+e^1+e^{-1} \\
& f(a)=1+2 e^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
(C)
$\begin{aligned}
& I_2=\frac{1}{3} \int_0^1 \frac{1}{e^t(2-t)} d t \\
& P u t x^3=t \Rightarrow 3 x^2 d x=d t \\
& I_2=\frac{1}{3} \int_0^1 \frac{1}{e^{1-1}(1+1)} d t \\
& \because \int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x \\
& \Rightarrow I_2=\frac{1}{3 e} \int_0^1 \frac{e^t}{1+1} d t \\
& \frac{I_2}{I_1}=\frac{1}{3 e} \Rightarrow \frac{I_1}{I_2}=3 e
\end{aligned}$
(D)
$\begin{aligned}
& I=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \sin (x-[x]) d(x-[x]) \\
& \because x=\{x \mid+\{x\} \\
& I=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \sin \{x\} d\{x\}=(-\cos \{x\})_0^{\pi / 4}
\end{aligned}$
Here $\frac{\pi}{4}$ and 0 are the limit of $\{x\}$, not $x$
$1=\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right)=1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.solution:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \int_0^{x^2 / 2} \frac{t^2}{x^2\left(1+t^2\right)} d t=\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ form
$\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\int_0^{x^2 / 2} \frac{t^2}{\left(1+t^2\right)} d t}{x^2}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{x^4}{4+x^4} x}{2 x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{x^5}{2 x^5+8}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: The value of $c \int_{1+c}^{a+c}[f(c x)+1] d x-\int_c^{a c} f\left(c^2+x\right) d x(c \neq 0)$, is equal to
questions.solution:
Putting $c x=c^2+z$
$c \int_{1+c}^{a+c} f(c x) d x=\int_c^{a c} f\left(c^2+z\right) d z$
So, the required value $=c \int_{1+c}^{a+c} d x=c(a-1)$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $I(a)=\int_0^a \ln (\cot a+\tan x) d x$, where $a \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ then $I(1)$ has value equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \int_0^a \ln (\cot a+\tan x) d x=\int_0^a \ln \left(\frac{\cos (a-x)}{\sin a \cdot \cos x}\right) d x \\ & =\int_0^a \ln \cos (a-x) d x-\int_0^a \ln \sin a d x-\int_0^a \ln \cos x d x \\ & I(a)=-\ln (\sin a) \cdot a \\ & I(1)=-\ln (\sin a)=\ln |\operatorname{cosec} 1|\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.solution:
$g(n)=\int_0^{n^2+n+1} e^{\{x / 2]}\left\{\frac{x}{2}\right\} d\{x\}$
$=\left(n^2+n+1\right) \int_0^1 e^{(x / 2)}\left\{\frac{x}{2}\right\} d x$
$=\left(n^2+n+1\right) \int_0^1 e^{x / 2}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) d x$
$=n^2+n+1\left[4-2 e^{1 / 2}\right]$
So, minimum value is $12-6 \sqrt{\mathrm{e}}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Let $F, F^{\prime}, F^{\prime \prime}$ be continuous in $\left[0\right.$, In 2] and $f(0)=0, f^{\prime}(0)=3, f(\ln 2)=6, f^{\prime}(\ln 2)=4$ and $\int_0^{\ln 2} e^{-2 x} f(x) d x=3$ then $\int_0^{\ln 2} e^{-2 x} f^{\prime \prime}(x) d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\int_0^{\ln 2} e^{-2 x} f^{\prime \prime}(x) d x$
$=\left[e^{-2 x} f^{\prime}(x)\right]_0^{\ln 2}+2 \int_0^{\ln 2} e^{-2 x} f^{\prime}(x) d x$
$=\left[\frac{1}{4} f^{\prime}(\ln 2)-3\right]+2\left[e^{-2 x} f(x)+2 \int e^{-2 x} f(x) d x\right]_0^{\ln 2}=13$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
If $\dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{2}{3!} + \dfrac{4}{5!} + \dfrac{6}{7!} + \ldots \infty$, then find the value of:

$\displaystyle\lim_{a \to 1^+} \int_a^x f(y)\, f'(y)\, \log_y(y^{\ln x})\, dy$

given that $f$ is a continuous, differentiable function whose graph passes through the point $(1, 0)$
questions.solution:
The given series is $\dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{2}{3!} + \dfrac{4}{5!} + \dfrac{6}{7!} + \dots \infty$

The $n^{\text{th}}$ term of the series can be written as $T_n = \dfrac{2n}{(2n+1)!}$

This can be simplified as $T_n = \dfrac{(2n+1) - 1}{(2n+1)!} = \dfrac{2n+1}{(2n+1)!} - \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)!} = \dfrac{1}{(2n)!} - \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)!}$

Summing the series from $n = 1$ to $\infty$ gives:

$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} T_n = \left(\dfrac{1}{2!} - \dfrac{1}{3!}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{4!} - \dfrac{1}{5!}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{6!} - \dfrac{1}{7!}\right) + \dots$

$\dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{1}{2!} - \dfrac{1}{3!} + \dfrac{1}{4!} - \dfrac{1}{5!} + \dfrac{1}{6!} - \dots$

Using the expansion $e^z = 1 + z + \dfrac{z^2}{2!} + \dfrac{z^3}{3!} + \dots$

Substituting $z = -1$, we get $e^{-1} = 1 - 1 + \dfrac{1}{2!} - \dfrac{1}{3!} + \dfrac{1}{4!} - \dfrac{1}{5!} + \dots$

Thus, $\dfrac{1}{x} = e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{e}$, which implies $x = e$

Now, consider the integral expression:

$\lim_{a \rightarrow 1^+} \int_a^x f(y) f'(y) \log_y(y^{\ln x}) \, dy$

The logarithmic term simplifies as $\log_y(y^{\ln x}) = \ln x \cdot \log_y(y) = \ln x$

Substituting $x = e$, we have $\ln x = \ln e = 1$

The expression becomes:

$\lim_{a \rightarrow 1^+} \int_a^e f(y) f'(y) \, dy$

Evaluating the integral gives:

$\int f(y) f'(y) \, dy = \dfrac{[f(y)]^2}{2}$

Applying the limits:

$\lim_{a \rightarrow 1^+} \left[ \dfrac{[f(y)]^2}{2} \right]_a^e = \lim_{a \rightarrow 1^+} \left( \dfrac{[f(e)]^2}{2} - \dfrac{[f(a)]^2}{2} \right)$

Since $f$ is a continuous function and its graph passes through $(1, 0)$, we have $f(1) = 0$

Taking the limit as $a \rightarrow 1^+$, $f(a) \rightarrow f(1) = 0$

The limit evaluates to $\dfrac{[f(e)]^2}{2} - 0 = \dfrac{[f(e)]^2}{2}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: If $f(x)=\int_0^4 e^{|t-x|} d t(0 \leq x \leq 4)$, the maximum value of $f(x)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\int_0^x e^{|t-x|} d t+\int_x^4 e^{|t-x|} d t=\int_0^x e^{x-t} d t+\int_x^4 e^{t-x} d t=-\left.e^{x-t}\right|_0 ^x+\left.e^{t-x}\right|_x ^4=e^x+e^{4-x}-2 \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=e^x-e^{4-x}=0 \Rightarrow x=4-x \Rightarrow x=2 \\ & f(0)=f(4)=e^4-1, f(2)=2\left(e^2-1\right) \text {, so maximum value of } f(x) \text { is } e^4-1\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
